3
Unix philosophy all the way. One tool, one job. Fail fast with clear error messages. Zero-config defaults — it works out of the box. Deterministic: same input, same output, every time. No network calls unless the user opts in. Composition over complexity — the tool should play nicely with pipes and other CLI tools.

logmunch — a structured log file analyzer and query tool. It reads JSON-lines log files (one JSON object per line), lets you filter, aggregate, and format the output. Think of it as jq meets grep for application logs. Target audience is backend developers and SREs who work with structured logging. Open source, MIT license. Used ad-hoc from the terminal or integrated into CI log analysis scripts.

Rust, using clap for argument parsing and serde for JSON. Testing with cargo test plus integration tests using assert_cmd and predicates. Distribution via cargo install and GitHub Releases with prebuilt binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows. Build with cargo.

logmunch is a multi-command CLI. Commands: query (filter and search logs), stats (aggregate statistics over fields), tail (follow a log file with filtering), format (convert between log formats), config (manage settings). query is the primary workhorse — supports field matching, time ranges, regex on values, and boolean combinators. stats computes count, min, max, avg, percentiles over a numeric field grouped by another field. tail is like tail -f but with the same filter syntax as query.

Reads JSONL files from disk or stdin. Each line must be valid JSON. Supports gzip-compressed files transparently. Environment variables: LOGMUNCH_CONFIG for config path, LOGMUNCH_FORMAT for default output format, NO_COLOR to disable colors. Config file at ~/.config/logmunch/config.toml. Precedence: CLI flags > env vars > config file > defaults. Malformed JSON lines are skipped with a warning to stderr and a count at the end.

Four output modes: text (human-readable, colored, columnar), json (re-emit matching lines as JSONL), csv (for spreadsheet import), table (aligned columns with headers). Controlled by --format flag. Stderr gets errors, warnings, and progress. Exit codes: 0 success with matches, 1 no matches found, 2 invalid arguments, 3 file not found or permission error, 4 parse error. Detects non-TTY stdout and auto-disables color and progress bars.

TOML config at ~/.config/logmunch/config.toml. Keys: default_format (text), color (auto/always/never), timestamp_field (timestamp), level_field (level), message_field (msg), max_memory_mb (512), pager (auto). Also supports project-local .logmunch.toml that overrides global. logmunch config show dumps current effective config, logmunch config set key value writes to global config.

Input JSONL is read line-by-line in streaming fashion. Each line is parsed to serde_json::Value. Filters are compiled into a predicate tree at startup and applied per-line. For stats, matching lines feed into an accumulator that tracks running counts, sums, min, max, and an online percentile sketch (t-digest). Memory usage is bounded — the tool streams and never loads the whole file. For gzip inputs, decompression is streamed via flate2. Parallelism: single-threaded for streaming, but stats aggregation could use rayon for multi-file queries in the future.

Errors go to stderr with a structured prefix: "error:", "warning:", "note:". Color-coded when on a TTY. Invalid arguments show the clap-generated help and suggest the closest matching command. File not found errors include the full resolved path. Verbose mode (--verbose or -v) shows: filter compilation details, lines scanned count, lines matched, processing time, memory usage. Debug mode (-vv) adds per-line parse timings. Unexpected panics produce a bug report template with version, OS, and reproduction steps.

Shell completion scripts generated by clap_complete for bash, zsh, and fish. Installed via logmunch completions bash > ~/.bash_completion.d/logmunch. Piping fully supported — designed to sit in the middle of a pipeline. Ctrl+C gracefully stops processing and still prints partial stats results. SIGTERM same. Color controlled by --color flag and NO_COLOR env var per the NO_COLOR spec. Progress bar shown on stderr during large file scans when stderr is a TTY.

Reads input files (never modifies them). Creates: ~/.config/logmunch/config.toml (on first logmunch config set), ~/.cache/logmunch/ for any internal caches. No lock files — the tool is stateless between invocations. No temp files in normal operation. If writing output to a file (--output flag), writes to a temp file in the same directory and atomically renames on completion.

Should process at least 500K JSONL lines per second on a single core for simple filters. Memory usage stays under 50MB for streaming queries regardless of input size. Stats mode uses ~10MB per unique group key. Gzip decompression adds ~30% overhead. No caching in v1 — every invocation reads from source. Future: index mode that builds a binary index for repeated queries over the same file.

Strict semver. CLI flags and command names are stable within a major version — no breaking changes in minor/patch releases. JSON output schema is versioned with a --schema-version flag if breaking changes are needed. Config file format is forward-compatible — unknown keys are ignored with a warning. Old config files work across minor version bumps.

cargo install logmunch for Rust users. GitHub Releases with prebuilt binaries for x86_64-linux, aarch64-linux, x86_64-macos, aarch64-macos, x86_64-windows. Homebrew tap: brew install logmunch/tap/logmunch. Future: apt repo for Debian/Ubuntu. No self-update in v1 — users use their package manager. Minimum: any OS with a 64-bit processor, no external runtime dependencies (static binary).

Unit tests for filter compilation, JSON parsing, stats accumulation, and config loading. Integration tests using assert_cmd: invoke logmunch with fixture files, assert stdout/stderr/exit code against expected snapshots. Fixture files in tests/fixtures/ with sample JSONL files covering: well-formed logs, malformed lines, empty files, huge lines, Unicode, nested JSON. Tested on Linux and macOS in CI via GitHub Actions. Performance benchmarks via criterion measuring lines-per-second for query and stats on a 1M-line fixture.

snake_case for all Rust code per standard conventions. Commands are single-word lowercase: query, stats, tail, format, config. Flags use kebab-case: --output-format, --timestamp-field, --max-results. Environment variables: LOGMUNCH_ prefix, UPPER_SNAKE: LOGMUNCH_CONFIG, LOGMUNCH_FORMAT. Domain terms: "record" means one JSON line, "field" means a JSON key, "filter" means a predicate expression, "accumulator" means a stats collector.

Config must contain: default output format, field name mappings (timestamp, level, message), color preference, memory limit, and pager setting. Config is TOML, loaded at startup by the config module. Complete default config: [output] format = "text", color = "auto", pager = "auto"; [fields] timestamp = "timestamp", level = "level", message = "msg"; [limits] max_memory_mb = 512; [ui] progress = true. Override hierarchy: compiled defaults < global toml < local .logmunch.toml < env vars < CLI flags. Reset via logmunch config reset or by deleting the config file.

Open questions: Should we support a query language beyond field filters (e.g., SQL-like SELECT with JOIN across files)? Punt until v2. Should tail mode support remote files over SSH? Defer. Should we add a TUI mode with scrollback and interactive filtering? Defer to a separate logmunch-tui crate. What's the right behavior when a field is missing from a JSON line — skip the line, treat as null, or error? Currently: treat as null (doesn't match any filter).

Not building yet: TUI interactive mode — CLI first, evaluate after v1.0. Plugin system for custom output formatters — need stable internal API first. SQL query language — too much scope for v1, revisit once basic filter syntax is validated in the wild. Remote file support (S3, SSH) — v1 is local files only. Index/cache system — need to understand real-world query patterns first.

s
